1 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

LLM TXTIn May 2026, Chrome Lighthouse quietly started checking every website for a file called llms.txt. A month later, Google’s own documentation said the file does nothing for search rankings. Both statements are true, and understanding why says more about where the web is headed than the file itself does.
Lighthouse’s new “Agentic Browsing” category isn’t measuring what Google Search measures. It’s asking a different question: can an autonomous browser agent — the kind that fills out forms and books things without a human watching — actually understand and use your page? llms.txt sits inside that category as one signal among several, next to accessibility-tree hygiene and a newer standard called WebMCP.
The practical finding worth knowing: most llms.txt “failures” aren’t missing files. A June 2026 audit of the web’s top 1,000 sites found 186 had attempted an llms.txt file, but only 64 passed validation. The single most common bug — confirmed independently by an SEO consultant’s own case study — is writing links as plain text instead of Markdown [text](url) syntax.
Full technical breakdown, a working file template, and the fix checklist, over on AI Thinker Lab: https://aithinkerlab.com/lighthouse-llms-txt-errors/
